title: Stacks & Queues
description: Stacks and queues underpin dozens of interview patterns — from valid parentheses to BFS traversal. Master stack<int>, queue<int>, and the standard templates so these problems become automatic.

sections.title: Stack operations
sections.explanation: **stack<int>** provides LIFO access with `.push()`, `.top()`, and `.pop()`. Note: `.pop()` returns void — read `.top()` first, then pop. Always check `.empty()` before calling top/pop. Interviewers expect the STL stack, not a hand-rolled one.

exercise.prompt: Push 5, 10, 15 onto a stack. Print the top element. Pop twice, printing the top after each pop. Then print the remaining stack size.

validation.message: Top starts at 15, then 10, then 5 after two pops, size = 1
Use .top() to peek, .pop() to remove, and .size() for the count

exercise.hint: Fill in top() to peek, then pop() removes the top. After two pops, one element (5) remains. Use size() for the count.

sections.title: Queue operations
sections.explanation: **queue<int>** provides FIFO access. Use `.push()` to enqueue at the back and `.front()` to peek at the front. `.pop()` removes the front element (returns void). Queues are essential for BFS — always reach for queue<int> when you see "level-by-level" or "shortest path in unweighted graph".

exercise.prompt: Enqueue values 100, 200, 300 into a queue. Print the front element. Dequeue once, print the new front, then print the remaining size.

validation.message: Front is 100, after one dequeue front becomes 200, size is 2
Use .front() to peek, .pop() to dequeue, and .size() for the count

exercise.hint: q.front() reads the first element. q.pop() removes it. After one pop, 200 is at the front with size 2.

sections.title: Valid parentheses pattern
sections.explanation: The classic stack problem: push every opening bracket onto the stack and, when you see a closing bracket, check that the top of the stack matches. If the stack is empty at the end with no mismatches, the string is valid. Use an **unordered_map** to store the bracket pairings.

exercise.prompt: Implement a valid parentheses checker. Test it on "({[]})" (should print true) and "{[}" (should print false).

validation.message: Use push to store opening brackets, top to compare, pop on match, empty to check at the end

exercise.hint: Push opening brackets with st.push(c). Compare st.top() with pairs[c]. Pop on match. Return st.empty() at the end.

sections.title: BFS with queue
sections.explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level using a queue. Start by enqueuing the source, then loop: dequeue the front node, process it, and enqueue all unvisited neighbors. Always use an **unordered_set** or visited array to avoid revisiting nodes. This pattern solves shortest-path problems on unweighted graphs.

exercise.prompt: Perform BFS on the graph {0: [1,2], 1: [3], 2: [3], 3: []} starting from node 0. Print each visited node separated by spaces.

validation.message: Use queue front/pop for FIFO and a visited set to avoid revisits

exercise.hint: q.empty() checks the loop condition. q.front() reads, q.pop() removes. Use visited.count(nbr) to check and visited.insert(nbr) to mark seen.

sections.title: Monotonic stack pattern
sections.explanation: A monotonic stack keeps elements in sorted order and is the key technique for **next greater element** problems. Iterate through the array: while the stack is non-empty and the top element is smaller than the current value, pop and record the answer. Then push the current index. This reduces O(n^2) brute force to O(n).

exercise.prompt: Find the next greater element for each item in {4, 5, 2, 10, 8} using a monotonic stack. Print the result vector space-separated.

validation.message: Use top() to peek at the index, pop() to remove, push(i) to add the current index

exercise.hint: st.top() peeks at the top index. Record result[st.top()] = nums[i], then st.pop(). Finally st.push(i).
